title: Criterion D — Presentation
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to mastering Criterion D: Presentation for the IB Extended Essay. It moves beyond a simple checklist to explain the academic rationale behind formal presentation, covering structural elements, academic honesty through referencing, and the effective use of visual aids to achieve the top markband.
learningObjectives: Understand the specific requirements of Criterion D (Presentation) for the Extended Essay.
Identify and correctly format all mandatory structural components of the EE, such as the title page and table of contents.
Apply a consistent academic referencing style for all in-text citations and the bibliography.
Learn how to integrate tables, graphs, and other visual elements to support the argument effectively.
Evaluate a draft EE against the top-band descriptors for Criterion D to identify areas for improvement.

simpleExplanation.title: The Academic Blueprint: Mastering EE Presentation
simpleExplanation.summary: Criterion D is not just about making your essay look neat; it is about presenting your research in a formal, scholarly, and clear manner that respects academic conventions. It demonstrates your professionalism and makes your complex arguments easy for the examiner to follow and assess.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your Extended Essay as a formal academic exhibit in a prestigious gallery. Your research and argument are the masterpiece painting (Criterion B & C). The presentation (Criterion D) is everything else: the elegant frame, the perfectly positioned lighting, the clear and informative label next to it, and the gallery guide that helps visitors navigate the space. Without these elements, even the best painting can be misunderstood, overlooked, or perceived as unprofessional.
steps: 1. Structure Audit: Systematically check for the presence and correct formatting of the title page, abstract, table of contents, page numbers, and section headings in the correct order.

2. Citation & Bibliography Check: Choose one consistent referencing style (e.g., MLA, APA) and meticulously verify that every in-text citation matches an entry in the bibliography, and that the bibliography itself is formatted perfectly.

3. Layout & Readability Review: Standardise your essay with a professional font (e.g., Times New Roman 12pt), double-spacing, and clear margins. Ensure all figures, tables, and graphs are numbered, titled, and referenced in the text.

4. The Final Proofread: Conduct a final, forensic-level proofread for spelling, grammar, and punctuation errors. Reading your essay aloud or having someone else read it can help catch mistakes that impede clarity.

flashcards.back: Assesses the extent to which the presentation follows standard academic format. Key elements include the title page, table of contents, page numbers, referencing, bibliography, and overall layout.
The presentation is effective, appropriate and clear. This means it aids communication and meets or exceeds standard academic expectations.
Must include: the essay title, the research question, the subject, and the total word count. The candidate's name, number, or school name should NOT be on the title page or anywhere in the essay.
Should list the main sections of the essay (Introduction, chapters/sections, Conclusion, Bibliography, Appendices) with correct page numbers. It provides a clear map of the essay's structure.
All pages, starting from the first page of the introduction, must be numbered consecutively. The title page, abstract, and table of contents should not have page numbers.
Using one single style (e.g., MLA, APA, Chicago) for all in-text citations, footnotes/endnotes, and the bibliography. Inconsistency is a common reason for losing marks.
A bibliography includes all sources consulted, even if not cited. A 'Works Cited' or 'References' list includes only the sources explicitly cited in the essay. The EE requires a bibliography.

Contain supplementary material that is not essential to the argument (e.g., raw data, interview transcripts, extensive calculations). They are not part of the word count and examiners are not required to read them.

The 4,000-word limit excludes: The Abstract, The Table of Contents, The Bibliography, and any Appendices. It includes the introduction, body, conclusion, and any footnotes that contain substantive argument.
A concise summary (max 300 words) of the research question, scope of the investigation, and conclusion. It is required but not formally assessed under any criterion.
All illustrations, tables, and graphs must have a number (e.g., Figure 1, Table 2), a descriptive title, and a source citation if not original. They must also be referred to in the body of the essay.

While not mandatory, they are highly recommended for clarity and structure in a long essay. They break the text into logical parts, guiding the reader through the argument. This contributes to a 'clear' presentation.

faq.q: Does the abstract count towards the 4,000-word limit?
faq.a: No, the abstract is excluded from the main word count. However, the abstract itself has a strict maximum of 300 words.

faq.q: Can I really lose marks for a few spelling or grammar mistakes?
faq.a: While one or two minor typos may be overlooked, an essay with numerous errors suggests a lack of care and proofreading. If these errors impede the clarity of your argument, you will certainly lose marks in Criterion D. A 'clear' presentation is largely free of such mistakes.

faq.q: What if my subject, like Mathematics, has different formatting conventions for equations?
faq.a: You should always follow any subject-specific guidance provided in the EE subject guide first. The general EE rules for presentation form the baseline, but disciplines like Mathematics or the Sciences have their own established conventions for presenting formulae, proofs, and data, which are considered 'appropriate' for that subject.

They are not strictly mandatory, but they are very highly recommended. For a 4,000-word essay, clear section headings are the most effective way to demonstrate a logical structure and guide the reader. Not using them makes it much harder to achieve a 'clear and effective' presentation.

faq.q: Do I need to include a source in the bibliography if I read it but didn't end up citing it in the essay?
faq.a: Yes. The EE requires a 'bibliography', not a 'works cited' list. A bibliography should include all sources you consulted during your research process that were influential to your thinking, even if you did not directly quote or paraphrase them in the final text.

sections.content: Criterion D, Presentation, is worth 4 marks and evaluates the formal structure and academic polish of your Extended Essay. While it may seem straightforward, achieving the top marks requires meticulous attention to detail. This criterion is not merely about aesthetics; it is a measure of your ability to communicate complex research within the established conventions of academic writing. A well-presented essay enhances the clarity of your argument and demonstrates respect for your reader, the examiner.
Deconstructing Criterion D: What Examiners Reward

The IB awards 3-4 marks for presentation that is 'effective, appropriate and clear'. Let's break this down:

*   **Effective:** The presentation actively helps the reader understand your argument. The structure is logical, headings guide the reader, and citations are easy to follow. Nothing is confusing or distracting.
*   **Appropriate:** The essay adheres to the conventions of academic writing for your chosen subject. This includes using a standard referencing style, formatting the bibliography correctly, and presenting data or images in a scholarly way.
*   **Clear:** The essay is easy to read and navigate. This relates to font choice, spacing, page numbering, and the correct labelling of all elements. It also means the essay is free from excessive spelling and grammar errors that obstruct meaning.

items: The goal of presentation is to support and clarify your academic argument, not to obscure it.
Examiners are looking for evidence of careful proofreading and adherence to formal academic standards.
A polished presentation signals to the examiner that you have taken the research process seriously from start to finish.
Failure to meet these standards can make a strong argument seem weak or difficult to follow, indirectly impacting other criteria.

sections.content:
Every Extended Essay must follow a specific structure. Missing or incorrectly formatting these elements will immediately limit your marks in Criterion D. The required order is:

1.  **Title Page:** Must contain your essay title, research question, subject, and word count. No personal details.
2.  **Abstract:** A summary of no more than 300 words.
3.  **Table of Contents:** A clear, navigable list of sections and their starting page numbers.
4.  **Introduction:** The first page of your essay's main body. Page numbering should start here (as page 1).
5.  **Body of the Essay:** Logically divided sections or chapters.
6.  **Conclusion:** Summarises the findings and answers the research question.
7.  **Bibliography:** A complete list of all sources consulted, formatted according to a single, recognised academic style.
8.  **Appendices (if used):** For supplementary, non-essential material.

sections.question: The following is a student's first draft of a Table of Contents for a History EE. Analyse its weaknesses in relation to Criterion D and explain how it should be improved.
sections.solution:
### Sample (Weak) Table of Contents:

*   Intro - page 1
*   Section 1 - page 3
*   Section 2 - page 8
*   Another bit - page 14
*   The end - page 18
*   Sources - page 19

### Analysis and Improvement:

This Table of Contents (ToC) fails to meet the 'clear and appropriate' standards of Criterion D for several reasons. Firstly, the section titles like 'Section 1' and 'Another bit' are generic and uninformative; they do not provide the reader with a conceptual map of the argument's progression. Secondly, the terminology is informal ('Intro', 'The end', 'Sources') instead of using standard academic terms like 'Introduction', 'Conclusion', and 'Bibliography'. Finally, the layout is basic and lacks the professional appearance of a formal academic document.

### Improved Table of Contents:

**Table of Contents**

Introduction....................................................................................1

1. The Political Climate in Pre-War Germany...................................3

2. The Reichstag Fire Decree and its Immediate Consequences......8

3. Consolidation of Power through Legislation: 1933-1934...........14

Conclusion.....................................................................................18

Bibliography..................................................................................19

**Justification:** This revised ToC is far more effective. The section titles are descriptive, outlining the specific focus of each part of the essay. This demonstrates a clear structure and helps the examiner follow the line of reasoning. It uses formal, appropriate language and a professional layout with dot leaders, which significantly enhances readability and meets the expectations for a scholarly piece of work, thus aligning with the top markband for Criterion D.

sections.content: Academic Honesty in Practice: Citations and Bibliography

A major component of Criterion D is the correct and consistent use of a recognised academic referencing style. This is not just a formatting exercise; it is a fundamental principle of academic honesty, allowing you to acknowledge your sources and enabling the examiner to verify them. You must choose one style (e.g., MLA 9, APA 7, Chicago 17) and apply it flawlessly throughout your essay.

items: **Consistency is paramount:** Mixing citation styles (e.g., using APA for some sources and MLA for others) is a major error.
**Check every detail:** Ensure every in-text citation corresponds to an entry in the bibliography, and vice versa.
**Format the bibliography perfectly:** The bibliography must be alphabetised by author's last name and formatted precisely according to your chosen style's rules for books, journals, websites, etc.
**Beware of generators:** Online citation tools can be helpful starting points, but they often make mistakes. You are responsible for manually checking and correcting every single entry.

sections.content: Decide on your referencing style the moment you begin your research. Use a reference management software like Zotero or Mendeley to keep track of sources, but always perform a final manual check against an official style guide (like the Purdue OWL website) before submitting your final essay. This diligence is what separates a 2-mark essay from a 4-mark one.

The overall readability of your essay is crucial. Small details collectively create a professional impression. Use a standard, legible font (e.g., Times New Roman or Arial, size 12) and double-space the entire main body of the text. This makes it easier for the examiner to read and annotate. Margins should be standard (e.g., 2.54 cm or 1 inch) on all sides.

Any visual elements like graphs, charts, or images must be integrated purposefully. They are not decorative. Each visual must be numbered (e.g., Figure 1, Table 1), given a clear, descriptive title, and be explicitly referenced and discussed in the main body of your essay. If the visual is from an external source, a citation must be included directly below it.

sections.question: A student in an Environmental Systems and Societies EE includes a bar chart showing CO2 emissions by country. It is pasted into the document without a label, number, or any reference in the text. Explain why this fails to meet Criterion D standards and write a short paragraph demonstrating how to correct it.
sections.solution:
### Analysis of Weakness:

Placing an unlabelled and unreferenced chart in the essay fails the 'clear and effective' test of Criterion D. The examiner does not know what the chart represents, where the data comes from, or why it is relevant to the argument. It is 'floating' data, disconnected from the analysis, which is a hallmark of poor academic presentation. The information, however useful, cannot be assessed and does not support the essay.

### Model Correction:

To correct this, the student must first label the visual appropriately and then integrate it into their written analysis. 

**(The chart would be placed here, with the following caption beneath it)**
*Figure 3: Total Annual CO2 Emissions by Country, 2021. Source: Global Carbon Project, 2022.*

**(The following paragraph would appear in the essay body)**
"The disparity in contributions to global greenhouse gas emissions is a critical factor in international climate policy negotiations. As illustrated in Figure 3, the total annual CO2 emissions from China and the United States far exceed those of other nations. For instance, China's emissions in 2021 were more than double those of the entire European Union combined, a fact that underscores the challenge of assigning equitable responsibility for climate change mitigation. This data directly supports the argument that..."

**Justification:** This correction is effective because the chart is now fully integrated. It has a clear number, a descriptive title, and its source is cited, fulfilling academic honesty requirements. Crucially, the text explicitly refers to 'Figure 3' and uses the data within it to build the argument. The presentation now actively enhances the analysis, meeting the top-band requirements for Criterion D.
